issues.claim: engineering layer's operational scoping of Sanskrit terminology
issues.objection: The engineering rendering of BG 2.56 narrows the Sanskrit's scope (Shankara's three-plane miseries gloss is acknowledged as commentarial expansion (the verse's literal scope is broader)) to a specifically operational frame. Acceptable simplification — the source pack preserves the fuller commentarial scope — but a careful Sanskritist notes the narrowing.

notes: The engineering layer for BG 2.56 preserves the verse's structural shape: Shankara's three-plane miseries gloss is acknowledged as commentarial expansion (the verse's literal scope is broader); the triad preserved as three distinct freedoms with engineering specifications. Ramanuja's analytic distinction (rāga as longing-for-not-yet-obtained; bhaya as affliction-from-anticipated-loss; krodha as disturbance-aimed-at-agent-of-loss) used to differentiate the three engineering pathologies — explicitly named in counter-example. Madhva's gloss (rasa as cognitive-error-of-pleasantness) compatible with analog's stack-attachment framing. No tradition is misattributed, no doctrinally load-bearing word is dropped without acknowledgment. The Sanskrit terms (sthita-prajña / sthita-dhīḥ / samādhi-sthasya / kūrma / rasa / param) appear preserved verbatim where invoked. PASS.
